coredata.dc:title: Synergistic improvement of antioxidant and antibacterial properties of carbon quantum complexes with zinc doping and chlorogenic acid for longan preservation
coredata.dc:creator: Du, Hongying
He, Linjing
coredata.dc:description: The deterioration of fruit could reduce the shelf life, decreased marketability and substantial economic value. Thus, a safe, simple, economical and environmentally friendly preservation technology for fruit is of great significance. Here, the postharvest preservation technology was investigated with zinc-doped carbon quantum dots and chlorogenic acid (Zn-CQDs/CGA) composite. Zn-CQDs/CGA composite were synthesized, which exhibits superior antioxidant and antibacterial activities. The binding mechanism of the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite was investigated, which revealed that the bindings of two components were mainly driven by hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces to create a novel composite. The Zn-CQDs/CGA composite was applied to longan preservation and was found to significantly reduce the incidence of mildew spot, browning of fruit endocarp and pulp, as well as the degree of degradation of quality indexes. These results suggest that the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite has the potential for inhibiting browning and preserving the quality of longan during storage.

head.ce:title: Synergistic improvement of antioxidant and antibacterial properties of carbon quantum complexes with zinc doping and chlorogenic acid for longan preservation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The deterioration of fruit could reduce the shelf life, decreased marketability and substantial economic value. Thus, a safe, simple, economical and environmentally friendly preservation technology for fruit is of great significance. Here, the postharvest preservation technology was investigated with zinc-doped carbon quantum dots and chlorogenic acid (Zn-CQDs/CGA) composite. Zn-CQDs/CGA composite were synthesized, which exhibits superior antioxidant and antibacterial activities. The binding mechanism of the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite was investigated, which revealed that the bindings of two components were mainly driven by hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces to create a novel composite. The Zn-CQDs/CGA composite was applied to longan preservation and was found to significantly reduce the incidence of mildew spot, browning of fruit endocarp and pulp, as well as the degree of degradation of quality indexes. These results suggest that the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite has the potential for inhibiting browning and preserving the quality of longan during storage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The longan (Dimocarpus Longan Lour.), originally from southern and southwestern China, is a well-known subtropical fruit with a juicy texture and sweet taste. It is currently grown and consumed in numerous countries and regions worldwide, including China, Southeast Asia, South Asia, Australia, and the United States (Chen et al., 2015). However, the fruit has a limited shelf life and is prone to spoilage when stored at room temperature for several days, with issues such as browning, microbial spoilage, and off-odors affecting its quality. Furthermore, harvesting and transportation during hot seasons can further hasten the deterioration of fruit, leading to reduced shelf life, decreased marketability and substantial economic value (Lin et al., 2017). Therefore, the research for a safe, simple, economical, and environmentally friendly longan preservation technology is of great significance in maintaining the quality of longan and extending its storage life.

The method of sulfur dioxide fumigation has been demonstrated to be an effective method for inhibiting decay and browning of longan fruit, and has been widely utilized in practical production (Joradol, Uthaibutra, Lithanatudom, & Saengnil, 2019). However, the potential risk of sulfur dioxide residues in longan fruit, which may cause carcinogenic risks and pose a hazard to human health, indicate the presence of significant side effects. Various countries and regions, including Europe, Japan and Australia, have enforced strict regulations on residual limits, while the United States only permits the use of sulfur dioxide in grape processing (Jiang, Zhang, Joyce, & Ketsa, 2002). In addition to chemical preservation, low-temperature storage, ozone treatment, modified atmosphere packaging, and film package have been utilized for longan and other fruit preservation. However, these methods have certain limitations, such as the presence of chemical residues, complex operational procedures, high instrument and equipment requirements.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a novel fluorescent nanomaterials since their discovery in 2004 (Xu et al., 2004), exhibiting exceptional properties such as photoluminescence, photostability, low toxicity, biocompatibility, antioxidant and antimicrobial properties. The outstanding characteristics of CQDs render them highly versatile for various applications, including fluorescence sensing, biological imaging, photocatalytic processes, and food preservation (He & Du, 2023). In recent years, the research of CQDs nanocomposites have garnered attention among researchers due to their potential to cater to the requirements of diverse fields. For instance, Wen et al. prepared carbon quantum dots/curcumin nanocomposites, which exhibited superior antioxidant activity compared to individual carbon quantum dots and curcumin. Furthermore, the antibacterial activity of curcumin was maintained. Therefore, the composite has the potential to be applied in the field of food preservation (Wen et al., 2023).

Polyphenols are secondary metabolites in plants with a diverse phenolic structure, widely distributed in various plant parts such as leaves, shells, and flesh (Zhu, 2021). Extensive research has shown that plant-derived polyphenols possess multiple biological activities, including antioxidant, antimicrobial, and chronic disease prevention, due to their unique chemical structure (Zhao et al., 2023). As natural product development and application continue to rise, plant polyphenols are increasingly gaining attention in the field of food science, due to their wide distribution in plants and good safety profile (Sinha, Sachan, Bhattacharya, Singh, & Parthasarathi, 2022). For instance, Su et al. treated litchi fruits with apple polyphenol solution. The polyphenol improved the antioxidant capacity of litchi fruits, reduced their respiratory rate, mass loss, and glucose and fructose content changes during storage, which helped to maintain the flavor and nutritional quality of litchi fruits (Su et al., 2019). Chlorogenic acid (CGA) is an organic compound formed by the combination of caffeic acid and quinic acid. It is considered to be one of the main phenolic compounds in plants such as coffee, honeysuckle, peach, apple, sunflower, pineapple, and blueberries(Upadhyay & Mohan Rao, 2013). In recent years, CGA has received significant attention from researchers due to its remarkable antioxidant properties and its potential benefits to human health, including antibacterial, anti-diabetic, blood pressure-lowering, neuroprotective, and cardioprotective effects (Santana-Galvez, Cisneros-Zevallos, & Jacobo-Velazquez, 2017). Currently, there exists a paucity of literature investigating the synthesis of composite incorporating CGA and CQDs.

This study utilized zinc-doped carbon quantum dots (Zn-CQDs) derived from crayfish shells and plant polyphenol CGA to prepare Zn-CQDs/CGA composites. The composites were characterized and investigated the interaction mechanism between the two components, and their antioxidant and antibacterial activities were also evaluated. Furthermore, the application of Zn-CQDs/CGA composites in longan preservation was explored, and the quality indexes of longan under different treatments (Zn-CQDs/CGA composites, CGA, and distilled water) were recorded and compared.

Crayfishes and longan were purchased from the local supermarket in Wuhan, China. CGA was obtained from Dalian Meilunbio Co., Ltd (Dalian, Liaoning, China). S. aureus (CMCC26003) and E. coli (CMCC44102) strains were purchased from Qingdao Hopebio Co., Ltd. (Qingdao, Shandong, China). LB broth medium, 2-thiobarbituric acid, trichloroacetic acid and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyphenol oxidase assay kit (colorimetric method) and peroxidase assay kit (colorimetric method) were purchased from Nanjing Jiancheng Biochemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, Jiangsu, China).

The synthesis of Zn-CQDs was performed using our previously established method. Initially, 0.548 g of zinc acetate dihydrate was dissolved in 25 mL of water to obtain a solution of zinc acetate. Following this, 1 g of crayfish shells powder was dispersed into the solution. The resulting mixture was then transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an air oven at 180 °C for 5.5 h. After air cooling, the brownish solution was centrifuged at 31,450 g (Avanti JXN-26, Beckman Coulter Life Science) for 30 min and filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe filter to eliminate sediments. The filtrate was subsequently dialyzed for 24 h using 1000 D dialysis membranes to further eliminate impurities, and the resulting solution was lyophilized for 24 h to obtain solid Zn-CQDs.

In this study, three Zn-CQDs/CGA composite powders were prepared with varying mass ratios of Zn-CQDs to CGA (2:1, 1:1 and 1:2). To achieve this, 100, 75 and 50 mg of Zn-CQD powders were separately dissolved in 45 mL of distilled water, while 50, 75 and 100 mg of CGA were dissolved in 5 mL of ethanol. The resulting solutions were mixed and stirred under darkness with magnetic stirring for 24 h to obtain a complex solution. Subsequently, the solution was freeze-dried for 24 h to obtain the desired composite powders.

Composites with a mass ratio of 1:1 were selected for characterization test. The functionalities of Zn-CQDs/CGA composites were identified by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectra in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using a Nicolet iS 50 Fourier transform infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo Scientific, USA). The UV–visible absorption spectrum was measured with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV2600A, Unico, China). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were obtained using a Bruker D8 X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). Thermogravimetric analysis was carried out using a NETZSCH TG 209F1 Libra thermogravimetric analyzer (NETZSCH, Germany) to investigate the thermal stability of the composites.

To investigate the binding mechanism of the interaction between Zn-CQDs and CGA, quenching fluorescent spectra were obtained at 298 K, 308 K, and 318 K using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm (the optimal excitation wavelength of Zn-CQDs). The concentration of Zn-CQDs was maintained at 2 mg/mL, and the CGA concentrations were varied from 0 to 100 μM.

For the DPPH scavenging test, 2 mL of Zn-CQDs/CGA composite samples with different concentrations (25, 37.5, 50 and 100 μg/mL) were added to 2 mL 0.2 mmol−1 DPPH solution to obtain the final solution (the concentrations of Zn-CQDs/CGA composites are 12.5, 25, 37.5 and 50 μg/mL). The mixture was incubated in the dark for 30 min, and the absorbance of both the DPPH solution (A0 ) and the sample mixture solution (Ai ) were measured at 517 nm. The percentage of DPPH free radical scavenging activity was evaluated using Equation (1):

For ABTS scavenging test, an ABTS assay solution was prepared by mixing 2.45 mmol−1 of potassium sulfate with 7 mmol−1 of ABTS solution and incubating it in the dark overnight. Then, 0.2 mL of Zn-CQDs/CGA composite samples with various concentrations (125, 250, 375 and 500 μg/mL) were added to 3.8 mL ABTS solution to obtain the final solution (the concentrations of Zn-CQDs/CGA composites are 6.25, 12.5, 18.75, and 25 μg/mL). The mixture allowed to incubate in the dark for 30 min. The absorbance of the ABTS solution (A0 ) and the ABTS solution with film sample (Ai ) were then recorded at 734 nm. The percentage of ABTS free radical scavenging activity was evaluated using Equation (2):

The Fe2+ chelating ability test was performed as reported method of with some modification (Klompong, Benjakul, Kantachote, Hayes, & Shahidi, 2008). Briefly, 0.1 mL of 2 mmol/L FeCl2 and 0.2 mL of 5 mmol/L ferrozine solution were added to 4.7 mL of Zn-CQDs/CGA composite solution (the concentrations of Zn-CQDs/CGA composites are 0.47, 0.94, 1.41, and 1.88 mg/mL). After reacting for 10 min at room temperature, deionized water was used to replace the composite solution as a blank, and the absorbance values of the blank and the sample were measured at 562 nm wavelength. The Fe2+ chelating ability was calculated using Equation (3):
(3)

Where As is the absorbance of the sample solution and Ai denote the absorbance of the blank solution.

The antibacterial was performed as reported method of with some modification (Yang, Lan, & Xie, 2022). The antibacterial property of the Zn-CQDs/CGA composites was evaluated by determining its minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) against Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus, which were determined by a microdilution method. Specifically, serial dilutions of composite, Zn-CQDs and CGA ranging from 20 to 0 mg/mL were prepared in triplicate in 96-well plates, and 100 μL of each concentration was added to the wells. Then, 100 μL of bacterial suspension containing 1 × 106 CFU/mL was added to each well, and the plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After incubation, the optical density of the bacterial culture was measured at 600 nm using a microplate reader.

The purchased longan fruits were harvested from the branches, followed by a gentle washing procedure in distilled water to remove extraneous soil and dust from the surface of the fruit. The washing process was terminated when the water became relatively clear. A total of 1050 longan fruits were randomized into three groups, each containing 350 fruits. In order to balance the antioxidant and antibacterial properties, the composites with mass ratio of 1:1 was selected in the preservation experiments. The fruits in each group were immersed separately in distilled water, 3 mg/mL chlorogenic acid solution and 3 mg/mL Zn-CQDs/CGA composite (with a Zn-CQDs to CGA mass ratio of 1:1) for 20 min. The treated fruits were then removed and left to air-dry naturally. The longan fruits were subsequently placed in a low-temperature constant incubator at 25℃ for 10 days, and the quality of the fruits was assessed on days 0, 2, 4, 6, 8, and 10 by randomly sampling fruits from each group.

In this study, observations and statistics were conducted on groups of longan fruit consisting of 150 each. Each group was divided into three parallel subgroups of 50 fruits each for analysis. The occurrence of visible mildew colonies on the surface of the longan fruit was considered as an incidence of mildew. The results were reported as the mean value of three parallel subgroups.

Ten longan fruit samples were randomly chosen from each group for the measurement of the endocarp and equatorial region of the fruit pulp. The color properties of the Zn-CQDs-PVA composite film were assessed using a Colorimeter (Shang Guang WSC-S, Shanghai, China) and recorded as L* (brightness), a* (redness-greenness), and b* (yellowness-blueness). The total color difference (ΔE) was calculated using Equation (4) (Mao et al., 2023):

Here, ΔL*, Δa*, and Δb* represent the differences in L*, a*, and b* values between the standard color plate and the samples, respectively.

To prepare the 2,6-dichlorindophenol sodium solution, 50 mg of the compound was dissolved in 200 mL of distilled water at 65 °C, followed by the addition of 52 mg of sodium bicarbonate. The resulting solution was cooled to 4 °C for 12 h, and then demarcated by ascorbic acid for subsequent use. Longan juice was obtained from 10 longan fruits (10 g) using 2 % oxalic acid (10 mL) and centrifuged at 6000 RPM for 15 min. The supernatant was titrated with the prepared 2,6-dichlorindophenol sodium solution. The content of vitamin C (VC) in the longan juice was calculated using equation (5):
(5)

Where the parameter N (mg/mL) was defined as the amount of 1 mL of dye solution equivalent to ascorbic acid. The volume of dye solution required for titration of longan juice solution was denoted as V1 (mL), while V0 (mL) was used to represent the volume of dye solution required for the titration of deionized water. Additionally, the total weight of the longan sample was represented by the parameter m (10 g).

Longan juice was prepared by homogenizing and filtering 10 g of longan fruits obtained from 10 longan fruits. The juice was then diluted with deionized water to a final volume of 100 mL. After a 30-minute incubation period, 10 mL of the longan juice solution was titrated with 0.02 M NaOH using phenolphthalein as an indicator. The titratable acidity was calculated using Equation (6):

Where C (0.02 mol/L) refers to the molarity of the NaOH solution, while the conversion factor F (g/mmol = 0.064) is used for citric acid (Zhou et al., 2021). The volume of NaOH solution used for the titration of longan juice solution is represented by V1 (mL), and V0 (mL) is used to denote the volume of NaOH solution used for the titration of deionized water. The volume of longan juice solution used for titration is represented by Vt (10 mL), while the total volume of longan juice solution is represented by V (100 mL). The total weight of the longan sample is represented by m (10 g).

The total soluble solids (TSS) of the longan fruits were measured using a portable refractometer (HT113ATC, YHT, China). To perform the measurement, the juice extracted from 10 longans was mixed and subjected to coarse filtration prior to testing.

The weight loss of longan fruits was determined using the gravimetric method. The weight of the longan fruit was recorded every other day, where 15 longans from each treatment group were divided into three parallel groups, and the same fruit was used for measurement throughout the entire experimental period. The percentage of weight loss was calculated using Equation (7):
(7)

Where W0 (g) is the initial weight of longan fruits and W1 (g) is the weight of longan fruits at 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10 day.

The method used in this study was adapted from reported method with slight modifications (Sun et al., 2011). A total of 10 g of longan flesh was extracted from 10 longan fruits and ground into a homogenous slurry using 3 mL of 10 % trichloroacetic acid solution. The slurry was then mixed with 7 mL of trichloroacetic acid solution and centrifuged at 4000 RPM for 15 min. A mixture of 3 mL of the resulting supernatant and 3 mL of 0.5 % 2-thiobarbituric acid solution was heated at 95 °C for 15 min, followed by immediate cooling in an ice-water. The resulting solution was centrifuged at 4000 RPM for 10 min, and the absorbance values at 532 nm, 600 nm and 450 nm were measured. The malondialdehyde content was calculated using Equation (8):
(8)

The measurement was conducted with slight modifications following the reported method (Duan, Jiang, Su, Zhang, & Shi, 2007a). 10 g of longan pulp were taken from ten longan fruits, and 5 mL of 80 % ethanol was used as the extraction solvent. The sample solution was prepared by grinding the pulp in ice-cold condition and centrifuging it at 6000 RPM. Then, 1.5 mL of the DPPH (0.1 mmol/L) ethanol solution was mixed with 1.5 mL of the sample solution. The mixture was incubated at room temperature in the dark for 30 min, and the absorbance was measured at 517 nm with 80 % ethanol as the blank. The percentage of DPPH free radical scavenging activity was calculated using Equation (10):

Where A0 is the absorbance of the blank DPPH solution at 517 nm and As is the absorbance of the sample solution at 517 nm.

The PPO activity was determined using the PPO test kit. The main steps involved in the test were extraction of crude enzyme solution, incubation with buffer and substrate solution, and measurement of absorbance at 420 nm. The PPO activity was calculated based on the fresh weight of the sample, and the unit of PPO activity was expressed as U/g tissue.

The POD activity was determined using the POD test kit. The main steps included crude enzyme extraction, incubation with the substrate solution, and measurement of absorbance at 420 nm. The POD activity was calculated based on the fresh weight of the sample, and the unit was expressed as U/g tissue.

The figures were drawn using Origin 2022 (OriginLab, USA). The statistical analyses were performed using Microsoft Excel (Microsoft, USA) and SPSS 22.0 (IBM, USA) and the significant differences between the means (P < 0.05) were determined by one-way ANOVA test. For all the studies, the values were presented as mean ± standard deviation (n ≥ 3).

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of Zn-CQDs/CGA composites, Zn-CQDs, and CGA are shown in Fig. 1
a. The spectrum of Zn-CQDs revealed that there are some functional groups such as COC, –OH and –CH groups on the surface of Zn-CQDs. The spectrum of CGA exhibits typical absorption peaks. The peaks at 3479 cm−1 and 3325 cm−1 are attributed to –OH groups, while the peak at 2960 cm−1 may be attributed to –CH2 groups. The peak at 1693 cm−1 is likely due to the stretching vibration of carbonyl groups, while the peak at 1524 cm−1 may be attributed to the benzene ring in the CGA molecule. The spectrum of Zn-CQDs/CGA composites shows the overlap of the spectra of Zn-CQDs and CGA. Upon composite formation, the –OH peaks of both Zn-CQDs and CGA exhibit significant shifts to 3412 cm−1, indicating the emergence of new hydrogen bonds between Zn-CQDs and CGA. In the fingerprint region of the spectrum, some characteristic peaks of the two original materials remain with positions and intensities changes, possibly due to the modification of functional group structure resulting from hydrogen bonding interactions (Liang et al., 2015). Therefore, it is inferred that the composites are mainly obtained by the interaction of hydrogen bonding.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of Zn-CQDs/CGA composite, Zn-CQDs, and CGA were presented in Fig. 1 b, which provides insights into the optical properties and interactions in the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite. The results revealed that CGA exhibited a typical absorption peak at around 334 nm, which can be attribute to aromatic ring in CGA. The Zn-CQDs/CGA composite exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at 324 nm, which may be attributed to the overlapping of the π → π* transition of the aromatic ring present in both Zn-CQDs and CGA structures. This overlap could account for the higher peak intensity at 324 nm observed in the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite. Furthermore, the peak intensity of Zn-CQDs at 265 nm in the spectrum of the composite decreased and shifted to 293 nm, which is possibly due to the formation of covalent bonds between the two components. This result is consistent with previous studies where covalent bonding between CGA and chitosan caused a red shift in the characteristic peak of the composite material (Jiao et al., 2019).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of Zn-CQDs, CGA, and Zn-CQDs/CGA composite are shown in Fig. 1 c. The XRD pattern of CGA exhibits several sharp peaks, which is characteristic features of polyphenol XRD spectra. The XRD pattern of Zn-CQDs is typical of CQDs, with a broad peak at 26.5°. The presence of many sharp peaks indicates that the material has a high degree of crystallinity, whereas the characteristic peaks of amorphous materials are mainly broad and diffuse (Jeddou et al., 2016). The XRD pattern of Zn-CQDs/CGA composite is similar to that of Zn-CQDs, with a broad peak at around 24°, indicating a decrease in crystallinity compared to CGA. Amorphous materials have better water solubility than materials with high crystallinity, which is consistent with the observed increase in solubility during the experimental process (Saavedra-Leos et al., 2014).

The thermal stability analysis of the composite system was performed using thermogravimetric curves, as depicted in Fig. 1 d. The obtained curves indicate that CGA experienced a first weight loss stage in the temperature range of 30–90 °C. This stage is mainly due to the evaporation of water, possibly caused by the thermal instability of CGA. Moreover, CGA undergone rapid decomposition between 220 and 450 °C, which corresponds to the major thermal degradation stage, where its mass loss reaches approximately 55 %. At this stage, the ester bond between quinic acid and caffeic acid, which constitute chlorogenic acid, is anticipated to break down initially with increasing temperature, producing carbon dioxide, followed by intramolecular dehydration and thermal decomposition of quinic acid and caffeic acid. The thermal decomposition of Zn-CQDs and Zn-CQDs/CGA composite is found to be more complex. In the temperature range of 30–400 °C, the Zn-CQDs curve is located above that of the composite, which could be attributed to the rapid thermal decomposition of CGA in the composite, resulting in a faster decomposition rate than that of Zn-CQDs. However, above 650 °C, the thermogravimetric curve of the composite shifts above the Zn-CQDs curve, indicating excellent thermal stability of the composite at this stage. These results suggest that the addition of Zn-CQDs enhances the thermal stability of the composite, while the composite's thermal stability is superior to that of Zn-CQDs at higher temperature.

To investigate the binding mechanism and types of fluorescence quenching between Zn-CQDs and CGA, the fluorescence spectra of Zn-CQDs quenched by CGA at different temperatures were analyzed. Fluorescence quenching can be classified into static quenching and dynamic quenching depending on whether stable complexes are formed. The two types of quenching can be distinguished by their Stern-Volmer quenching constant. In static quenching, the quenching constant decreases with increasing temperature, while in dynamic quenching, the opposite is observed. The fluorescence quenching of CGA on CS-CQDs can be described using the Stern-Volmer equation (Equation (11) (Lakowicz & Weber, 1973):
(11)

Where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of Zn-CQDs in the absence and presence of the CGA, respectively. The quenching rate constant is represented by Kq , and [Q] denotes the concentration of CGA. The average fluorescence lifetime of CQDs is denoted by τ0 , and for the Zn-CQDs, τ0 is measured to be 1.03 ns. Additionally, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant is denoted by KSV .

The Stern-Volmer equations at different temperatures (298, 308, and 318 K) demonstrated a good linear relationship and were shown in Fig. 1 e. The slopes of the three lines were found to vary with increasing temperature, indicating that the temperature affects the interaction between Zn-CQDs and CGA. From Table 1 , it can be observed that both the KSV and Kq values gradually decreased with increasing temperature. The corresponding Kq values at the three temperatures were calculated to be 1.62 ± 0.029 × 1014 (298 K), 1.46 ± 0.013 × 1014 (308 K) and 1.36 ± 0.009 × 1014 (318 K) L/mol/s, all of which were higher than the maximum diffusion collision quenching rate constant of variety of bovine serum albumin quenchers 2.0 × 1010 L/mol/s (Shen, Liu, Wang, Jiang, & Shi, 2015). This suggests that the fluorescence quenching caused by CGA is a static quenching process. Hence, the interaction between the quencher CGA and Zn-CQDs led to the formation of a ground state complex that lacked fluorescence. This complex could absorb light and then relax to the ground state without emitting any photons, thus causing a quenching in the fluorescence of Zn-CQDs.

The binding interaction between Zn-CQDs and CGA was analyzed using the double logarithmic equation (Equation (12):
(12)
l g
F 0
/ F - 1
= l g
K a
+ n l g
Q
Where Ka represents the binding constant and n denotes the number of binding sites.

The values of the Ka and n were shown in Table 1. It was found that the Ka value decreases with increasing temperature, indicating that the stability between Zn-CQDs and CGA decreased with temperature, and their binding became increasingly difficult. The n values at the three temperatures were all close to 1 and decreased with increasing temperature, suggesting that Zn-CQDs bind to CGA at a single binding site, and the binding also becomes more difficult with increasing temperature.

An analysis of the thermodynamic parameters is necessary to determine the binding forces involved in the Zn-CQDs/CGA system. Ligands can interact with molecules through several types of binding forces, including hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic bonding, electrostatic, and van der Waals forces (Fathi et al., 2019). The hydrophobic interaction in this system was characterized by ΔH > 0 and ΔS > 0. Negative values of ΔH and ΔS suggested the presence of hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces. Additionally, electrostatic forces were indicated by ΔH < 0 and ΔS > 0 (Yu et al., 2022). To determine the thermodynamic parameters, the Van't Hoff equation can be used (Equation (13) and (14):
(13)
lg
K a
= -
( Δ H / R ∙ T )
+ Δ S / R
(14)
Δ G = Δ H - T Δ S
Where R represents the gas constant with a value of 8.314 J/mol/K. The absolute temperature is denoted by T, and the enthalpy change is represented by ΔH. The change in free energy is ΔG, while the change in entropy is designated by ΔS.

The thermodynamic parameters of the binding reaction between Zn-CQDs and CGA are listed in Table 1. Both ΔH (-34.2 kJ/mol) and ΔS (-11.7 J/mol/K) are negative, indicating that Zn-CQDs may interact with CGA mainly through van der Waals forces and hydrogen bonding, which is consistent with the conclusion inferred from the FT-IR analysis. This may be due to the fact that Zn-CQDs contain abundant functional groups such as –OH and –COOH that serve as hydrogen bond donors and form hydrogen bonds with the nine hydrogen bond acceptors on the CGA molecule.

The Fig. 2
a-2b demonstrated that the DPPH radical scavenging abilities of Zn-CQDs/CGA composites with varying Zn-CQDs: CGA mass ratios of 2:1, 1:1, 1:2, Zn-CQDs and CGA, all increased continuously with increasing concentration. Notably, the addition of CGA significantly enhanced the DPPH radical scavenging rate of the composites compared to Zn-CQDs, and the scavenging rate increased continuously with increasing CGA ratio, indicating that the addition of CGA can substantially improve the antioxidant activity. Similar results were observed for the ABTS radical scavenging rate (Fig. 2 c-2d), where the abilities of Zn-CQDs, Zn-CQDs/CGA composites and CGA to scavenge ABTS radical all increased with increasing concentration and proportion of CGA. The superior radical scavenging ability of CGA may be attributed to its two highly reductive phenolic hydroxyl groups, which also enhance the free radical scavenging ability of Zn-CQDs after combination.

Furthermore, transition metals such as Fe2+ can catalyze oxidation in lipids and proteins and are considered important pro-oxidants (Agrawal, Joshi, & Gupta, 2016). As depicted in Fig. 2 e, the Fe2+ chelation abilities of the three composites with were significantly greater than those of Zn-CQDs and CGA and gradually decreased with increasing CGA ratio in the composites. At a concentration of 1.41 mg/mL, there was no significant difference (P > 0.05) among the three composites. These results indicated that the combination of Zn-CQDs and CGA has increased their antioxidant abilities and suggest that the two have formed new composites instead of simple physical mixture.

Fig. 3 d-3e depicts the inhibitory activity of Zn-CQDs and CGA against Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli). Zn-CQDs shown a higher inhibitory activity with a MIC value of 0.15625 mg/mL, compared to CGA, whose MIC value is 0.3125 mg/mL. However, the antibacterial activity of the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite shown a decreasing trend with an increase in the proportion of CGA, which leads to a decrease in the MIC values ( Fig. 3
a-3c). The MIC values of the 2:1, 1:1, and 1:2 composites were 0.15625, 0.3125, and 0.3125 mg/mL, respectively.

Fig. 3 f-3j presents the inhibitory effects of Zn-CQDs, CGA, and Zn-CQDs/CGA composites on Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus). The MIC value of Zn-CQDs against S. aureus is 0.07813 mg/mL, which is slightly lower than that against E. coli. The MIC value of CGA against S. aureus is 0.625 mg/mL. Similar to the results for Escherichia coli, an increase in the proportion of CGA in the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite leads to a decrease in its inhibitory activity against S. aureus.

These results suggest that Zn-CQDs possess better antibacterial activity than CGA, and Zn-CQDs/CGA composite exhibit certain antibacterial activity. Moreover, an increase in the proportion of Zn-CQDs in the composite enhances its antibacterial activity, with the MBC of the 1:1 complex against both E. coli and S. aureus being 2.5 mg/mL. Previous research has reported that grafting other substances to CGA can improve its antibacterial activity. For instance, Yang et al. grafted CGA with chitosan to obtain chitosan/CGA composites for inhibiting S. aureus, with MIC value of 0.15625 mg/mL (Yang et al., 2022).

As depicted in Fig. 5 a, longan fruits in all groups showed varying changes after 10 days of storage. These changes were mainly characterized by the appearance of mildew spots on the endocarp and browning of the pulp, which could be attributed to microbial invasion and enzymatic browning of the fruits. It can be seen from the figure that mildew spots appeared on the surface of fruits in all control groups, while only a few fruits in the Zn-CQDs/CGA group showed mildew spots. Additionally, the color of the pulp in the control group had turned dark brown, indicating severe fruits browning, while the number of fruits with obvious browning in the other two treatment groups was relatively small. These results demonstrate the efficacy of Zn-CQDs/CGA in maintaining the visual quality of longan fruits.

The incidence of mildew spots ( Fig. 5 b) revealed that the occurrence of mildew spots in longan fruits continued to increase in all three groups during the storage period. In the first two days, only two longan fruits showed mold spots, and there was no significant difference among the three groups (P > 0.05). However, on the fourth and sixth day of storage, the number of moldy fruits in the control group began to increase rapidly, with a significant difference compared to the two treatment groups. By the eighth day of storage, there was a significant difference between the CGA treatment group and the Zn-CQDs/CGA treatment group. After 10 days of storage, the incidence of mildew in the control group was 95.3 %, while that in the CGA group was 50.7 %, and that in the composite group was 31.3 %, with significant differences observed among all groups. These results suggest that Zn-CQDs/CGA composite have a significant effect in preventing microbial invasion of longan fruits.

The color changes of longan pulp and endocarp during storage can be quantified using three stimulus color systems, such as L value (lightness), a value (greenness/redness) and b value (blueness/yellowness). The decrease in L value indicates continuous darkening of the endocarp and pulp, whereas an increase in a value indicates reddening, an increase in b value indicates yellowing. Besides, an increase in ΔE value suggests a stronger color difference between standard whiteboard and longan endocarp and pulp.

As shown in Fig. 4
a-4d, the color of the longan pulp changed during storage, with a continuous decrease in L value and a continuous increase in a value, b value, and ΔE. This indicates that the color of the longan pulp darkened during storage, and tended towards more red and yellow, which exacerbate the browning of the pulp. After 8 days of storage, the control group and the two treatment groups showed significant differences (P < 0.05) in all values, indicating that CGA and the composite had a significant effect in inhibiting the browning of the longan pulp. The endocarp of longan displayed a similar trend during storage ( Fig. 4 e-4h), with a decrease in L value and an increase in a value, b value, and ΔE. The endocarp's color became darker, redder, yellower. Similarly, after 8 days of storage, the control group and the two treatment groups showed significant differences (P < 0.05) in all values, indicating that CGA and the composite had a significant effect in inhibiting the browning of the longan endocarp.

In other studies, polyphenols and CQDs have been used as functional materials to preserve and inhibit browning in fruits. For instance, Bai et al. employed different polyphenol solutions, including anthocyanins, tea polyphenols, apple polyphenols and chlorogenic acid to soak litchi fruits and discovered that all types of polyphenols had a significant inhibitory effect on litchi browning (Bai et al., 2022). In another study, Su et al. fabricated a CQDs/albumin preservation film for litchi preservation, which significantly reduced the rate of decline in L* value of litchi fruits (Su et al., 2023).

VC content, total soluble solids content, titratable acidity content, and weight loss rate during storage are important indexes to evaluate the quality characteristics of fresh longan after harvest and storage (Jiang et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 5 c, the VC content of three groups decreased with storage period. From the fourth day of storage, the VC content of the two treatment groups showed significant differences (P < 0.05) from that of the control group, indicating that the two treatment groups significantly delayed the decline of VC content in longan fruits.

As shown in Fig. 5 d, the soluble solids content of three groups of longan fruits showed a decreasing trend during storage. The decrease in soluble solids content may be due to the decrease in carbohydrates and pectin content and the hydrolysis of proteins and glycosides during the respiration process of the fruit (Li et al., 2015). Significant differences between the three groups appeared from the second day of storage, indicating that the use of CGA and Zn-CQDs/CGA composite could significantly delay the decline in soluble solids content of longan fruit during storage.

As shown in Fig. 5 e, the titratable acidity of longan fruits showed a trend of initial decrease followed by an increase during storage. In the early stage of storage, titratable acidity was rapidly decomposed into carbon dioxide and water due to the respiration of the fruit, resulting in a rapid decrease. However, in the middle and late stages of storage, the titratable acidity content began to increase, which may be due to the proliferation of microorganisms (such as Geotrichum candidum) in the fruit, which produced acidic metabolites, resulting in an increase in total acidity (Chen et al., 2020). During the decrease, the control group had the fastest rate of decline, while the composite group had the slowest rate of decline, with significant differences appearing from the second day of storage. Then the titratable acidity content began to increase. Although there was no significant difference between the three groups on the 10th day, the titratable acidity content of the control group had already higher than that of the two treatment groups, indicating that the control group was more severely invaded by microorganisms.

As shown in Fig. 5 f, the weight loss rate of longan fruits continued to increase during storage, with the control group having a faster rate of increase than the two treatment groups. On the 10th day of storage, the weight loss rate of the control group was 21.3 %, while the weight loss rates of the CGA and composite groups were 14.7 % and 14.8 %, respectively.

The above results indicate that the use of CGA and Zn-CQDs/CGA composite can significantly inhibit the quality decline of longan fruits during storage. The antioxidant properties of CGA and Zn-CQDs/CGA composite may reduce the damage of reactive oxygen species to fruit cells and improve the fruit's antioxidant capacity. In addition, their antibacterial properties may reduce the invasion of microorganisms on longan fruits and inhibit the rapid increase in respiration rate caused by adversity stress, thereby slowing down the decline of index such as VC content, total soluble solids content, weight and also reducing the increase of titratable acidity caused by microorganisms’ acidic products. Furthermore, the weight loss rate of longan fruits may be influenced by factors beyond those mentioned. Specifically, it is possible that CGA and Zn-CQDs/CGA composite solutes that remain on the fruit surface after evaporation could form a barrier that slows down the process of water loss and weight loss in longan during storage.

The capacity of DPPH radical scavenging reflects the inherent ability of longan fruit cells to resist the oxidation of reactive oxygen species. As demonstrated in Fig. 5 g, the longan fruit's DPPH radical scavenging rate exhibited a decreasing trend during the 10-day storage period. The control group displayed a gradual decrease in DPPH radical scavenging ability within the first two days, followed by a rapid decline, possibly due to the activation of the longan's reactive oxygen species scavenging system in the first two days, followed by the disruption of the balance of reactive oxygen species in the subsequent days. The treatment group's decline was comparably slower than that of the control group, and there was a statistically significant difference (P < 0.05) between the two treatment groups and the control group on the 4th day of storage. Treatment with polyphenols and polyphenol composites raised the total phenol content of the longan fruit, thereby augmenting its radical scavenging capacity and maintaining the balance of reactive oxygen species in longan, resulting in the protection of the fruit. Zhang et al. reached similar conclusions when treating litchi fruit with apple polyphenols The litchi treated with polyphenols exhibited significantly higher DPPH radical scavenging ability than the control group litchi (Zhang et al., 2015).

During storage, the malondialdehyde (MDA) content in the control and treatment groups was recorded ( Fig. 5 h). The MDA content in all groups increased during the 10-day storage period, with a slower increase during the first four days, followed by a rapid increase. By the 10th day of storage, the MDA content in the control group (2.43 μmol/g) was significantly higher (P < 0.05) than that in the CGA treatment group (2.11 μmol/g) and the composite treatment group (2.10 μmol/g).

The accumulation of reactive oxygen species during adversity stress in plants can lead to lipid peroxidation and damage to the plant cell membrane system. The final product of this peroxidation reaction is MDA, which is an important indicator for evaluating lipid peroxidation and cell membrane integrity (Tang et al., 2021). Longan fruit quality can be compromised by damage to the cell membrane system during storage, leading to enzymatic browning and decreased antioxidant capacity. MDA can also bind to proteins in plant tissue, altering protein structure and enzyme conformation. In this study, the significantly lower MDA content in the treatment groups suggests that CGA and Zn-CQDs/CGA treatments can effectively remove oxygen free radical and protect the integrity of the longan cell membrane system. This can delay browning and quality deterioration in longan by maintaining the regional distribution of browning-related enzymes and substrates in cells. Other studies have also shown a relationship between MDA content and fruit quality, such as the reduction of MDA accumulation and maintenance of post-harvest peach fruit quality by CGA treatment (Xi et al., 2017).

During the storage period of longan fruit, the levels of PPO activity in the control group and different treatment groups were investigated and presented in Fig. 5 i. As storage time increased, PPO activity in all groups of longan fruit continued to increase. The control group exhibited a rapid increase in PPO activity from the 4th day of storage. By the 6th day, a significant difference (P < 0.05) was observed between the control group and the two treatment groups. Similarly, both treatment groups experienced a rapid increase in PPO activity from the 6th day, but by the 10th day, the PPO activity of the control group remained significantly higher than that of the CGA and Zn-CQDs/CGA treatment groups.

PPO is one of the main enzymes involved in enzymatic browning of fruits. It catalyzes the oxidation of phenols to quinone, which then polymerize to make longan fruit appear dark brown (Shi et al., 2013). Active free-state PPO is primarily distributed in the cytoplasm, while phenols in plant cells are typically present in vacuoles. Thus, the cell membrane system can localize active PPO and substrate phenols within the cell, preventing their contact and avoiding the reaction. However, harvesting and subsequent storage of longan fruit can lead to aging and damage, as well as the rapid production of reactive oxygen species under adversity stress conditions, which can damage the integrity of the cell membrane system and destroy the localized distribution. This can allow phenols in the vacuole to come into contact with the enzyme in the cytoplasm, resulting in fruit browning. Additionally, bound PPO can gradually transform into active PPO, leading to a continuous increase in total PPO activity.

The results of this study indicate that treatment groups significantly suppressed PPO activity, thereby inhibiting the quality decline and browning of longan fruit. This may be attributed to the good antioxidant properties of CGA and Zn-CQDs/CGA composition, which may have helped the longan cell membrane system resist the damage caused by reactive oxygen species, delaying the destruction of the cell membrane system. This is supported by the changes in the content of MDA previously mentioned. Furthermore, it is worth noting that the properties of polyphenol oxidase differ in various plants. Jiang's research revealed that CGA and polyphenol oxidase in longan do not interact with each other (Jiang, 1999). Therefore, the addition of CGA and Zn-CQDs/CGA may not exacerbate the polyphenol oxidation reaction catalyzed by polyphenol oxidase.

During the storage period, the enzymatic activity of POD in longan fruit of the control group and different treatment groups was presented in Fig. 5 j. Similar to the PPO trend, the POD activity increased continuously throughout the entire storage period. From the 4th day of storage, the POD activity in the control group was found to be higher than that of the two treatment groups, displaying a significant difference.

POD is an oxidoreductase widely present in plant tissues, capable of catalyzing the oxidation of phenolic substances by hydrogen peroxide, leading to browning of longan fruit when exposed to hydrogen peroxide. Similar to PPO, the active free form of POD is primarily located in the cytoplasm. In this study, compared to the control group, the two treatments significantly inhibited the activity of POD. This phenomenon can be attributed to the excellent antioxidant properties of CGA and Zn-CQDs, which can help longan cell membrane systems resist the damage caused by reactive oxygen species. Consequently, POD would be unable to catalyze the reaction between phenolic substances and hydrogen peroxide.

Similar studies have demonstrated examples of inhibiting the enzymatic activities of PPO and POD to alleviate longan browning and deterioration in quality. For instance, Duan et al. treated longan fruit with nitric oxide donors and observed that PPO and POD enzyme activities in the treatment group were lower than those of the control group. Additionally, the browning index's rate of increase was also reduced in the treatment group (Duan et al., 2007b).

Zn-CQDs/CGA composite were synthesized from CGA and Zn-CQDs, which exhibits superior antioxidant (DPPH and ABTS free radical scavenging rate and Fe2+ chelation rate) and antibacterial activities against E. coli and S. aureus. The binding mechanism of the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite was investigated, revealing that the fluorescence quenching of Zn-CQDs by CGA was mainly due to static quenching, and the two components were primarily bound by hydrogen bonding and van der Waals forces to create a novel composite. The Zn-CQDs/CGA composite was employed for the preservation of longan fruit, resulting in a marked reduction in the incidence of mildew, browning of the fruit endocarp and pulp, and a decline in quality indexes such as VC content, titratable acid content, soluble solids content, and weight loss rate. Additionally, it effectively suppressed the deterioration of DPPH free radical scavenging ability and MDA production in longan, as well as the activities of polyphenol oxidase and peroxidase. These findings suggest that the Zn-CQDs/CGA composite exhibits significant potential for inhibiting browning and preserving the quality of longan fruit during storage.

Hongying Du: Supervision, Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Linjing He: Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft.
